--- execute: echo: true categories: [Power BI, PowerQuery, Excel, intermediate] params: sl_date: 2025-09-17 fn: "Groups and summaries in PowerQuery" id: KP23 format: html date: "2025-09-17" title: "Groups and summaries in PowerQuery" --- ::: {.content-visible unless-format="revealjs"} ::: {.cell layout-align="left"} ## Previous attendees have said... - 7 previous attendees have left feedback - 100% would recommend this session to a colleague - 86% said that this session was pitched correctly ![](group_summarise_power_query_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){fig-align='left' width=288} :::{.callout-note} ### Three random comments from previous attendees - useful worked examples to follow along with - Found it very useful, well paced and opportunities to ask no daft questions. - Informative and well presented as usual, thanks Brendan. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.content-visible when-format="revealjs"} ## Forthcoming sessions ```{.r .cell-code} KINDR::training_sessions(tr_type = "Power Platform", start_date = params$sl_date, n = 5, hide_area = T) ``` `````{=html}
Booking Link Materials Date Level
Conditions in Power Automate Session materials 10:00-12:00 Tue 23rd September 2025 🌶🌶: intermediate-level
Mailing lists in Power Automate Session materials 10:00-12:00 Tue 30th September 2025 🌶🌶: intermediate-level
Approvals in Teams and Power Automate Session materials 10:00-11:00 Tue 7th October 2025 🌶🌶: intermediate-level
Scope of the possible with PowerQuery Session materials 10:00-11:00 Tue 13th January 2026 🥬: pre-beginner-level
PowerQuery - a practical introduction Session materials 10:00-12:00 Wed 18th February 2026 🌶 :beginner-level
````` ::: ## Introduction This session will: - introduce the concept behind groups and summarising - work through a single-group summary to illustrate the core workflow - extend that to more complex summary processes to illustrate the scope of the possible - and spend a little bit of time reviewing some core PQM knowledge useful for those looking to group and summarise in the real world ## Setup - please download the [HACA2023-4 survey data](https://www.gov.scot/binaries/content/documents/govscot/publications/statistics/2024/05/health-and-care-experience-survey-2023-to-2024-results-by-geography/documents/health-and-care-experience-survey-2023-to-2024-geographical-data/health-and-care-experience-survey-2023-to-2024-geographical-data/govscot%3Adocument/Health%2Band%2BCare%2BExperience%2BSurvey%2B2023%2Bto%2B2024%2B-tables%2Bof%2Bresults%2Bby%2Bgeography.xlsx) and open in Excel - then import the `Information questions` sheet into PowerQuery using `Get data from Table/Range`
(or press `Alt`, `A`, `P`, `T`) ![Get data from Table/Range](../src/images/clipboard-2183037797.png) That should open your data in PowerQuery: ![PowerQuery window](../src/images/pq_first_sight.png) ## Big idea - grouping and summarising is possible and desirable in PowerQuery - assuming that your analysis can begin and end with the summarised data - if you need a mix of full- and summarised-data, consider doing something different (like a Pivot table or DAX formula) - and Pivot tables are a good way of understanding PQ's approach to summarising data: take some data, possibly split by other variables, and apply a summary function - it has the advantage that it returns standard Excel data, rather than oddball Pivot tables, and is comparatively easy to learn ## Basic workflow We'll follow the same basic set of steps in each example: 1. duplicate your Query and rename 2. put the data into groups 3. then apply a summary function to produce a new summarised table of data 4. then return to ordinary PowerQuery work on that new table ## First summary - duplicate your `Table1` query and rename to `geography_type` - select the `Geography Type` column, then press `Group by` ![Group by](../src/images/clipboard-3037087040.png) - then select the function that you'd like to use to summarise - we'll start with `Count rows` ![Count rows](../src/images/pq_count_rows.png) - that will return a summarised table where we've created a new column (`Count`) that contains a summary measure - a count of rows - for each of the groups that we specified (`Geography Type`) ![Summarised data](../src/images/pq_summarised.png) ## Sum the summary: total responses per question text While it can be useful to count rows, we can also use the summary function to total up aspects of our data for us. - repeat the previous steps - so create a new query named `total_responses`. Then select `Group by`, and populate as follows: ![Sum values](../src/images/pq_total_responses.png) That will produce output that summarises the total responses given to each question: ![Summed values per question](../src/images/pq_total_responses_out.png) ## Levels of grouping Our two examples so far have just used a single layer of grouping. But we could be more ambitious by adding extra grouping variables. - duplicate to `multi_layers` and `Group by` again - switch to `Advanced` mode in `Group by`, then `Add grouping` - repeat the `total_responses` summary work from the previous example as per the screenshot below: ![Levels of grouping](images/clipboard-3920954063.png) - that should yield a similar sum of responses, but this time the groups will be per-area and per-question ![Mult-layer grouping](../src/images/pq_total_responses_multi.png) ## Levels of aggregation You can also, with the help of the `Advanced` mode, add several aggregation to your summary table. Rename your query to `multi_aggregation`, select `Group by` and set the options as follows: ![Multiple aggregations](../src/images/pq_total_responses_multi_agg.png) That should yield multiple summary columns with values for each group: ![Multiple aggregation results](../src/images/pq_total_responses_multi_agg_out.png) ## Understanding PQM While most of the time we can use the menu, it's extremely useful to have a basic idea about how the underlying PowerQuery M code works, especially if you think you might be using non-standard summary measures. Setup a new query named `concat_questions`, grouping by `Area Name` and again summing the number of responses in a new column called `total_responses`. Then apply the following settings: ![Total responses by area name](../src/images/pq_total_responses_custom_start.png) Once that grouping is applied, look at the formula bar, where you should see an PQM expression: ![Total responses by area name](../src/images/pq_total_responses_pqm.png) `= Table.Group(#"Changed Type", {"Area Name"}, {{"total_responses", each List.Sum([Number of responses to question]), type nullable number}})` This means that we can start to tweak the PQM expressions to gain more control over groups and summaries. Approximately: - `{"Area Name"}` is the grouping variable. If you update this to `{"Area Name", "Question Number"}`, it'll add the question number as a second layer of grouping - `"total_responses"` is the name of the new column - `each List.Sum([Number of responses to question])` describes how the aggregation (sum) takes place. Try tweaking this to `each List.Average([Number of responses to question]),` - `type nullable number` describes the type of data the summary column contains. Try updating to `type text` - next, try a different function. In place of `List.Average([Number of responses to question])`, try `Text.Combine(List.Transform([Question Text], Text.From), "_")` - finally, add a step to de-duplicate your results ::: {.content-visible when-format="revealjs"} ## Forthcoming sessions ```{.r .cell-code} KINDR::training_sessions(tr_type = "Power Platform", start_date = params$sl_date, n = 5, hide_area = T) ``` `````{=html}

````` ## Feedback Please can you [spare us one minute of your time to give feedback](%60r%20KINDR::feedback_url(params$fn,%20date%20=%20params$sl_date)%60)? :::
